ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pig intestine food product class; no specific braised preparation subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers pig intestine as offal; no braised-preparation facet applied. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for braised pig intestine as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised pig intestine is approximately 150 g, as served in a Malaysian or Singaporean Bak Kut Teh or braised pork restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, large intestine, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for braised pork large intestine used as the primary nutrient anchor. Malaysian-style braised preparation adds soy sauce and spices; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical braising liquid absorption (~600–700 mg/100 g). All other macros retained from USDA SR Legacy braised pork intestine values. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not contain a specific braised pig intestine entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-quality approved source available for this food.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods: pork offal and organ meats — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked meat products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pig intestine in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 32 based on the established principle that high-protein, high-fat, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat and offal products have low GI values (typically 20–40) due to negligible starch content. This is consistent with GI values reported for other braised pork and offal dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 150 g serving = (32 × 1.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.58, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid braised pig intestine due to its high sodium content (~650 mg/100 g), which can contribute substantially to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg; a 150 g serving provides approximately 975 mg sodium (~49% of the daily limit).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised pig intestine has a very low carbohydrate content (~1.2 g/100 g) and a low estimated glycemic index (~32), resulting in a negligible glycemic load per serving (~0.6), making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; however, the high fat and cholesterol content warrants moderation in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who also have dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised pig intestine is high in dietary cholesterol (~143 mg/100 g), saturated fat (~4.6 g/100 g), and sodium (~650 mg/100 g), all of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with existing cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should consume this food only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised pig intestine (卤猪粉肠, Lu Zhu Fen Chang) is a traditional Chinese and Malaysian-Singaporean dish consisting of cleaned pig small intestine slow-cooked in a soy-sauce-based braising liquid typically containing dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, five-spice powder, garlic, ginger, sugar, and water. The dish is a classic component of Bak Kut Teh (pork rib tea soup) menus and braised pork (lor bak) stalls across Malaysia and Singapore. The intestine is first thoroughly cleaned and blanched to remove impurities, then braised for an extended period until tender and deeply flavoured. The texture is soft, slightly chewy, and gelatinous, with a rich umami flavour imparted by the soy-based braising sauce. Nutritionally, braised pig intestine is a high-fat, moderate-protein, very-low-carbohydrate food. Per 100 g it provides approximately 168 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 13.5 g fat (of which 4.6 g is saturated), and only 1.2 g carbohydrate. It is notably high in dietary cholesterol (~143 mg/100 g) and sodium (~650 mg/100 g due to soy sauce absorption), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or dyslipidaemia. Its glycemic index is low (~32) and glycemic load per serving is negligible (~0.6), so it does not significantly raise blood glucose. It is not halal as it is a pork product. In Singapore and Malaysia it is commonly served as a side dish or as part of a Bak Kut Teh set meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in braised pig intestine?
Is braised pig intestine high in cholesterol?
Can diabetics eat braised pig intestine?
What does braised pig intestine taste like?
How is Lu Zhu Fen Chang prepared?
Is braised pig intestine high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Malaysian-style braised pig intestine exists in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy braised pork large intestine (NDB 10397), which is the highest-quality approved source available. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect soy-sauce braising absorption typical of Malaysian preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, high-fat, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy braised pork large intestine; sodium adjusted to ~650 mg/100 g to reflect Malaysian soy-sauce braising. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as intestinal tissue contains no dietary fibre; this is naturally zero for this food type. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; organ meats contain negligible vitamin C after cooking. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for braised pork intestine. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this specific preparation. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for braised pork intestine. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for braised pork intestine. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this preparation. 9. GI is an analogous estimate of 32 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database reference principles for high-protein, high-fat, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat and offal; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 10. GL per 150 g serving = (32 × 1.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.576, rounded to 0.6. 11. This food is not halal (pork product). 12. FoodOn match is closest_parent (pig intestine food product); no braised-preparation subclass exists. 13. FoodEx2 A0CKP is closest_parent for pig intestine offal; no braised facet applied. 14. SNOMED CT has no suitable food product concept for braised pig intestine.
